content.full_text:
COUNTY OF SAN DIEGO

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL CORRESPONDENCE

Date October 31, 1975

TO: Registrar of Voters

A 95-192

FROM: County Counsel

RE: Committees and campaign statement filing requirements
(County Counsel Assignment No. 75-1309)

You requested answers to three questions concerning activities
of committees organized to support candidates or measures and the
filing of campaign statements. We discuss these questions separately
below, the first two of which are answered by reference to the
Political Reform Act of 1974 (Title 9 of the Government Code, hereafter referred to as the "Political Reform Act") inasmuch as the
"Hairman-Dymalley Campaign Disclosure Act (hereafter referred to as
"Proposition 9") which is Division 8 of the Elections Code became
inoperative for most purposes on June 28, 1975 as a result of enactment
of Chapter 145 of the 1975 Statutes.

Your first question is as follows:

"1. Is a committee supporting a candidate or
measure in an election routinely terminated after the
past election campaign statement is filed without
notifying the Secretary of State? What are the
acceptable and lawful dispositions of surplus funds
when a committee is terminated?"

Government Code Section 84101* requires a statement of
organization to be filed by each committee (as defined in
Section 82013) with the Secretary of State and Section 84102
prescribes the contents of that statement; the statement must include
"the disposition of surplus funds which will be made in the event of
dissolution" (of the committee). (Section 84102.) The Political
Reform Act does not otherwise specifically refer to
the dissolution of a committee but Section 84103 requires that
whenever "there is a change in any of the information contained in
a statement of organization, an amendment shall be filed within ten
days to reflect the change". Campaign statements must be filed
within specified dates after an election (Section 84200 and 84202) and
"closing dates" are established by Section 84205. Thus, if a
committee is to be dissolved because of having no additional
functions to perform it is considered implicit in the provisions
of the Act that the Secretary of State should be so notified. The

*Hereinafter all Section references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noticed.

Register of Voters                    -2-                    October 31, 1975


disposition of surplus funds would have to be accomplished
in a manner consistent with the requirements of the statement
of organization pursuant to Section 84102(f). We have no basis
for rendering legal advice as to what might be "acceptable and
lawful dispositions of surplus funds" and it is up to the
committee to determine the appropriate dispositions; presumably if a
contributor considered the disposition not to be proper he would
have to challenge the committee's actions by legal proceedings of
his own instigation.

Your second question is as follows:

     "2. Is a committee which has supported candidates
in several counties and in several elections and has
filed statements with the Secretary of State and the
counties involved required to continue filing with all
these offices even though subsequent statements may
report only office expenses or the support of one or two
local candidates? Must it continue multiple filings during
its existence?"

Section 81005 contains requirements for filing reports and statements
of candidates and committees with the Secretary of State and
with the clerks of particular counties. The key language is that as long as a
committee is in existence it must make the required reports to
the Secretary of State and to the clerks of the counties specified
because the committee must remain constituted as set forth in its
statement of organization which includes the information required
by Section 84102 until a change in that information occurs and an
amendment is filed as required by Section 84103 or the committee
is dissolved. In other words once a committee has been organized
it retains its structure and purpose as established by its statement
of organization on file with the Secretary of State and must continue
to file the reports and statements required by Section 81005 appropriate
to its organization until an amendment to the statement of organization
has been filed in accordance with Section 84103 or the committee
has been dissolved.

Your third question is as follows:

     "3. Is a committee organized under the Waxman
Dimally Disclosure Law [sic] considered to be a
continuing committee after January 7, 1975 if it has
not disposed of surplus funds and is it required to
continue 'off year' campaign reporting?"

Registrar of Voters                -3-                October 31, 1975

Waxman-Dymally continued in force until June 28, 1975, the effective
date of Chapter 145 of the 1975 Statutes which added Elections
Code Sections 11620, 11621 and 11622, and as of that date became
inoperative except as to activities covered by the act which occurred
prior to January 7, 1975 and to specified campaign expenditures.
Therefore, a committee organized under that act which was in
existence on June 28, 1975 would be considered to have ceased to
function by operation of law on that date except with respect to
activities which are specified in Elections Code Sections 11620
and 11621; Section 11621 provides that "Every candidate and committee
which received contributions or made expenditures prior to January 7,
1975, and as of the closing date of the previous campaign statement,
and every elective officeholder shall file a campaign statement
not later than July 31, 1975, completing the disclosure requirements
under this chapter",(i.e. Chapter 1 of Waxman-Dymally, Elections
Code Sections 11500-11622), and the periods to be covered by the
campaign statement are specified in the section. Thus a committee
is a "continuing committee" after January 7, 1975 at least for the
purposes of Elections Code Sections 11620 and 11621 and of course
it would have to take appropriate measures for disposition of any surplus
funds. In view of the provisions of Elections Code Section 11620
it would appear that your question as to "off-year" campaign reporting
is no longer pertinent because Elections Code Section 11553.5 which
affords to such reporting is no longer effective; Section 84206 covers
such a situation with respect to a committee organized under the
Political Reform Act.

If the above discussion does not fully respond to your
questions please so advise us and we will be pleased to provide
additional advice.

Very truly yours,

ROBERT G. BERRY, County Counsel

By [signature]
ROBERT B. HUTCHINS, Deputy

RBH:gh

We discuss these questions separately below, the first two of which are answered by reference to the Political Reform Act of 1974 (Title 9 of the Government Code, hereafter referred to as the "Political Reform Act") inasmuch as the Waxman-Dymally Campaign Disclosure Act (hereafter referred to as "Waxman-Dymally") which is Division 8 of the Elections Code became inoperative for most purposes on June 28, 1975 as a result of enactment of Chapter 145 of the 1975 Statutes. Your first question is as follows: "1. Is a committee supporting a candidate or measure in an election routinely terminated after the post election campaign statement is filed without notifying the Secretary of State? What are the acceptable and lawful dispositions of surplus funds when a committee is terminated?" Government Code Section 84101* requires a statement of organization to be filed by each committee (as defined in Section 82013) with the Secretary of State and Section 84102 prescribes the contents of that statement; the statement must include "the disposition of surplus funds which will be made in the event of dissolution" [of the committee]. (Paragraph (f) of Section 84102.) The Political Reform Act does not otherwise specifically refer to the dissolution of a committee but Section 84103 requires that whenever "there is a change in any of the information contained in a statement of organization, an amendment shall be filed within ten days to reflect the change". Campaign statements must be filed on specified dates after an election (Sections 84200 and 84203), and "closing dates" are established by Section 84205. Thus, if a committee is to be dissolved because of having no additional functions to perform it is considered implicit in the provisions of the Act that the Secretary of State should be so notified.
